1: Holy war theology (Deuteronomy 20:1). אַל-תִירָא (do not fear) — because YHWH goes with them. כִּי-יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ עִמָּךְ (for YHWH your God is with you) — the divine presence as the decisive military factor. Horses and chariots (סוּסִים וְרֶכֶב) were the ancient arms race; Israel’s king was explicitly forbidden to multiply horses (Deuteronomy 17:16). The asymmetric warfare presumption: YHWH fighting for Israel defeats any numerical/technological disparity.
2: The priest addresses the troops (Deuteronomy 20:2). וְנִגַּשׁ הַכֹּהֵן (the priest shall step forward) — the military context requires a priestly officiant. Holy war is a sacred event. The priest speaks on YHWH’s behalf before battle begins, in contrast to Israel’s later failure to consult YHWH before engaging battle (Numbers 14:44-45).
3: Hear, O Israel (Deuteronomy 20:3). The battle address begins שְׁמַע יִשְׂרָאֵל (Hear, O Israel) — echoing the Shema’s opening (Deuteronomy 6:4). The four-fold prohibition: do not let your heart grow soft, do not fear, do not panic, do not be terrified. The quadruple prohibition addresses the psychological dimensions of pre-battle fear comprehensively.
4: YHWH fights for you (Deuteronomy 20:4). כִּי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם הַהֹלֵךְ עִמָּכֶם (for YHWH your God is the one who goes with you) — the divine warrior motif. לְהוֹשִׁיעַ אֶתְכֶם (to save/give victory) — yasha, the root of “Joshua/Jesus/Yeshua.” The Song of the Sea (Exodus 15:3) is foundational: “YHWH is a warrior.”
5: New house exemption (Deuteronomy 20:5). חֲנֻכָּה (chanukah, dedication) — the same word as the festival of Hanukkah (Numbers 7:10-11). A man who has built a house but not yet dedicated it is exempt from service. The exemptions in Deuteronomy 20:5-7 are expressions of the covenant blessings of land, vine, and family — Israel fights precisely to enjoy these goods, not to be denied them by premature death.
6: Vineyard exemption (Deuteronomy 20:6). חִלֵּל (chillel, make common/begin to use) — the first three years of a fruit tree’s produce are orlah (Leviticus 19:23); year four is sacred; year five is normal use. To plant a vineyard and die before enjoying it is a covenant curse (Deuteronomy 28:30); the exemption protects the warrior from this irony.
7: Betrothed exemption (Deuteronomy 20:7). אֵרַשׂ (eras, betrothed) — legal commitment not yet consummated. Betrothal in ancient Israel was binding (breaking it required divorce, Matthew 1:19). The man is exempted “so he does not die and another man take her” — the same formulation as Deuteronomy 28:30’s covenant curses reversed.
8: The fearful exemption (Deuteronomy 20:8). יָרֵא וְרַךְ הַלֵּבָב (afraid and soft/faint-hearted) — the fearful soldier is sent home so he does not cause others to “melt” (יִמַּס, yimas) in their heart. The psychological realism is notable — the law accommodates human weakness rather than demanding impossible heroism from all.
9: Commanders appointed (Deuteronomy 20:9). שָׂרֵי צְבָאוֹת (sarei tzeva’ot, commanders of the armies) — appointed after the exemptions are processed, so the fighting force is determined before leadership is assigned. The order — screen the army, then command it — reflects practical military wisdom embedded in the legal framework.
10: Offer of peace to distant cities (Deuteronomy 20:10). קָרָאתָ אֵלֶיהָ לְשָׁלוֹם (call to it for peace/summons it to peace) — שָׁלוֹם (shalom, disp=2) here means “terms of peace” or “a peace arrangement” (MKT “offer it peace”), not merely absence of war but formal submission. The requirement to offer terms before siege applies only to “distant cities” (Deuteronomy 20:15) — the Canaanite cities are explicitly excluded (Deuteronomy 20:16-18).
11: If it accepts (Deuteronomy 20:11). Accepting the peace terms means the city opens its gates and submits to מַס (mas, forced labor/tribute/corvée) — compulsory labor service. The MKT “compulsory labor” reflects the labor obligations imposed on subjugated peoples throughout the ancient Near East (cf. Solomon’s labor levy, 1 Kings 5:13-14). Submission means labor servitude but not slaughter.
12: Siege if refuses (Deuteronomy 20:12). לֹא-תַשְׁלִים עִמָּךְ (does not make peace with you) — the same root as shalom. If the city refuses peaceful submission, siege (צוּר, besiege/hem in) follows. The siege is coercive — bringing the city to terms by military pressure.
13: Sword to all males (Deuteronomy 20:13). וְהִכִּיתָ אֶת-כָּל-זְכוּרָהּ לְפִי-חָרֶב (strike all its males with the edge of the sword) — standard ancient Near Eastern practice for resisting cities. This applies to distant cities, not the Canaanite cities under cherem. The males represent the city’s military and resistance capacity.
14: Plunder of distant cities (Deuteronomy 20:14). The women, children, livestock, and goods — שָׁלָל (shalal, plunder/spoil) — may be taken as booty for Israel. This is standard ancient warfare law for non-cherem cities. The war laws contain humanitarian constraints even within the violence they regulate.
15: Distant cities (Deuteronomy 20:15). The rules in Deuteronomy 20:10-14 apply specifically to cities “far from you, not belonging to these nearby nations.” The distinction is crucial: the leniency applies only to non-Canaanite peoples. The six nations in Deuteronomy 20:17 are under an entirely different law, cherem, for the specific theological reasons stated in Deuteronomy 20:18.
16: Nothing alive (Deuteronomy 20:16). לֹא תְחַיֶּה כָּל-נְשָׁמָה (do not let anything that breathes remain alive) — נְשָׁמָה (neshamah) is the breath of life (Genesis 2:7). The absoluteness of the cherem for the listed nations is theologically motivated, not ethnic animus — the reason is given explicitly in Deuteronomy 20:17-18.
17: Cherem (Deuteronomy 20:17). הַחֲרֵם תַחֲרִימֵם (utterly devote to destruction) — the infinitive absolute intensification of charam. חֵרֶם (cherem) is sacred destruction: the thing is removed from ordinary use and consecrated entirely to YHWH’s wrath (cf. 1 Samuel 15:3, Saul’s failure with the Amalekites). The six peoples listed are the standard Canaanite roster (cf. Genesis 15:20-21). MKT “utterly destroy them” captures the intensified verbal form.
18: Reason for cherem (Deuteronomy 20:18). לְמַעַן אֲשֶׁר לֹא-יְלַמְּדוּ (so that they do not teach you) — the purpose clause explains the total destruction: the Canaanite cult would corrupt Israel’s covenant relationship. תּוֹעֲבֹתֵיהֶם (their abominations) echoes the occult list of Deuteronomy 18:9-14. The cherem is a prophylactic measure against religious contamination, not ethnic genocide per se.
19: Do not destroy fruit trees (Deuteronomy 20:19). לֹא-תַשְׁחִית אֶת-עֵצָהּ (do not destroy its trees). The rhetorical question: כִּי הָאָדָם עֵץ הַשָּׂדֶה — “Is the tree of the field a person/man?” — is interpreted: (a) the tree is not your enemy, so do not make war on it; (b) you may eat from it (because it is not an enemy soldier). MKT “for is the tree a person, that you should besiege it?” captures the rhetorical sense. A remarkable humanitarian-ecological constraint embedded in warfare law.
20: Non-fruit trees (Deuteronomy 20:20). Non-fruit-bearing trees (אֲשֶׁר לֹא-תֵדַע, which you know — yada, disp=2: “know/recognize” that they do not bear fruit) may be cut for siege-works. מָצוֹר (matsor, siege works/fortifications) — the timber is repurposed for the military objective. The distinction fruit-bearing/non-fruit-bearing creates a proportionate environmental rule: destroy only what serves the military purpose, preserve what sustains life.
